front: What is the primary goal of exploring a data distribution?
back: The primary goal is to understand the underlying structure, patterns, and properties of the data, such as its central tendency, spread, skewness, and the presence of outliers, before applying formal modeling techniques.

An empirical distribution is a distribution function based on the observed data points from a sample, as opposed to a theoretical distribution. It is described by the empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF).

front: What is the difference between a unimodal and a multimodal distribution?
back: A unimodal distribution has a single clear peak or mode, indicating one frequent value. A multimodal distribution has two or more clear peaks, suggesting the data may be composed of multiple distinct subgroups or processes.

front: Why is identifying skewness important in machine learning?
back: Skewness (a measure of asymmetry) is important because many statistical models and machine learning algorithms assume data is normally distributed. High skewness can violate this assumption, leading to poor model performance, and often requires transformation (e.g., log transform).

front: What information does a Quantile-Quantile (Q-Q) plot provide?
back: A Q-Q plot is a graphical tool used to assess if a sample dataset comes from a theoretical distribution (e.g., normal). It plots the quantiles of the sample data against the quantiles of the theoretical distribution. Points falling on a straight line suggest the distributions are similar.
